title: Adrenergic Receptors
summary: المستقبلات الأدرينالية هي مستقبلات الجهاز العصبي الودي التي تستجيب للنورإبينفرين والإبينفرين. تنقسم إلى أنواع رئيسية: ألفا-1 (تقبّض الأوعية) وألفا-2 (تثبيط قبل شبكي) وبيتا-1 (تنشيط القلب) وبيتا-2 (ارتخاء العضلات الملساء كالشعب الهوائية) وبيتا-3 (المثانة والأنسجة الدهنية). العقاقير المنبّهة تحاكي تأثيرات الجهاز الودي، بينما الحاصرات تعاكس هذه التأثيرات وتُستخدم في علاج ارتفاع ضغط الدم وفشل القلب والربو وغيرها.

key_drugs.name: Phenylephrine
key_drugs.note: مضيّق أنفي ومعالج لانخفاض الضغط - يسبب بطء قلب انعكاسي

key_drugs.name: Prazosin / Doxazosin
key_drugs.note: لارتفاع ضغط الدم - خطر إغماء أول جرعة (First-dose Syncope)

key_drugs.name: Tamsulosin
key_drugs.note: لتضخم البروستاتا الحميد (BPH) - يحسن تدفق البول

key_drugs.name: Clonidine
key_drugs.note: يخفض ضغط الدم مركزياً - الانسحاب المفاجئ يسبب ارتفاع ضغط دم ارتدادي

key_drugs.name: Phenoxybenzamine
key_drugs.note: للورم القواتمي (Pheochromocytoma) - لا رجعة فيه - دائماً قبل حاصر بيتا

key_drugs.name: Phentolamine
key_drugs.note: للورم القواتمي وأزمات الضغط - ترياق لتسرب Norepinephrine الوريدي

key_drugs.name: Dobutamine
key_drugs.note: لفشل القلب الاحتقاني - يزيد الانقباض والناتج القلبي

key_drugs.name: Metoprolol / Atenolol
key_drugs.note: لارتفاع الضغط وفشل القلب والـ COPD - انتقائية بيتا-1 بالجرعات المنخفضة فقط

key_drugs.name: Esmolol
key_drugs.note: للحالات الحرجة - نصف عمر 9 دقائق - وريدي فقط

key_drugs.name: Propranolol
key_drugs.note: لارتفاع الضغط والرجفان الأذيني والصداع النصفي والرعاش - ممنوع في الربو

key_drugs.name: Labetalol / Carvedilol
key_drugs.note: Labetalol لارتفاع الضغط في الحمل - Carvedilol لفشل القلب

key_drugs.name: Mirabegron
key_drugs.note: للمثانة فرطة النشاط (OAB) - يُرخّي عضلة الدتروسور

mnemonics.title: توزيع مستقبلات بيتا: 1-2-3
mnemonics.body:
Beta-1 = 1 قلب (One Heart)
Beta-2 = 2 رئتان (Two Lungs) + أوعية العضلات الهيكلية
Beta-3 = 3 حروف 'Fat' (الأنسجة الدهنية + المثانة)
قاعدة: Beta-1 ينبّه القلب - Beta-2 يُرخّي الأوعية والشعب

mnemonics.title: Alpha-1: 'اضغط واعصر'
mnemonics.body: Alpha-1 = تقبّض (Squeeze). تذكّر: Alpha-1 يعصر الأوعية فيرتفع الضغط. حاصره يوسّع الأوعية فيهبط الضغط. أول جرعة Prazosin تسبب إغماء لأن الضغط ينخفض فجأة - لهذا تُعطى عند النوم.

mnemonics.title: Clonidine وارتداد الضغط
mnemonics.body: تخيّل Clonidine كـ'صمام' يُغلق مركزياً إفراز النورإبينفرين. إذا نزعت الصمام فجأة (توقف مفاجئ) - النورإبينفرين ينفجر ويسبب ارتفاع ضغط ارتدادياً خطيراً. دائماً تحذير: لا توقف Clonidine فجأة.

mnemonics.title: موانع حاصرات بيتا: ABS
mnemonics.body:
A = Asthma (ربو - ممنوع تماماً لأن Beta-2 مسؤول عن توسع الشعب)
B = Bradycardia شديدة
S = Severe AV Block
السكري: حذر - حاصرات بيتا تُخفي أعراض نقص السكر (ما عدا التعرق)

mnemonics.title: Phenoxybenzamine قبل Beta Blocker دائماً
mnemonics.body: في ورم القواتم - الإبينفرين يحفز Alpha وBeta. إذا أعطيت حاصر بيتا أولاً - تثبط Beta-2 الموسّع للأوعية فيبقى Alpha-1 حراً يُسبب انقباضاً وأزمة ضغط قاتلة. القاعدة الذهبية: Alpha blocker FIRST - ثم Beta blocker.

flashcards.front: What is the Alpha-1 receptor and what is its main effect?
flashcards.back: Gq-coupled receptor — found in blood vessels and the bladder — effect: smooth muscle contraction (vasoconstriction and blood pressure elevation)

flashcards.front: What is the Alpha-2 receptor and where is it located?
flashcards.back: Gi-coupled receptor — located on the presynaptic neuron — inhibits Norepinephrine release (negative feedback)

flashcards.front: What is the effect of Beta-1 stimulation?
flashcards.back: Increases heart rate (HR), contractility, conduction, and Renin secretion — via Gs-cAMP

flashcards.front: What is the effect of Beta-2 stimulation?
flashcards.back: Smooth muscle relaxation: bronchodilation, dilation of skeletal muscle vessels, uterine relaxation

flashcards.front: What is Phenylephrine and what are its uses?
flashcards.back: Alpha-1 agonist — used as: (1) nasal decongestant, (2) IV treatment of severe hypotension, (3) eye drops for pupil dilation

flashcards.front: What is 'First-dose Syncope' and with which drug does it occur?
flashcards.back: Fainting at the first dose due to sudden blood pressure drop — classic with Prazosin (Alpha-1 blocker) — first dose is recommended at bedtime

flashcards.front: What is the difference between Prazosin and Tamsulosin?
flashcards.back: Prazosin: Alpha-1B blocker — acts on vessels — used for hypertension. Tamsulosin: Alpha-1A blocker — acts on prostate and bladder — used for benign prostatic hyperplasia (BPH)

flashcards.front: How does Clonidine lower blood pressure?
flashcards.back: Central Alpha-2 agonist — reduces Norepinephrine release from the central nervous system — decreases sympathetic tone

flashcards.front: What is the danger of abrupt discontinuation of Clonidine?
flashcards.back: Severe rebound hypertension that may be life-threatening

flashcards.front: What is the difference between Phenoxybenzamine and Phentolamine?
flashcards.back: Phenoxybenzamine: non-selective irreversible Alpha blocker. Phentolamine: non-selective reversible Alpha blocker

flashcards.front: Why must an Alpha blocker be given before a Beta blocker in pheochromocytoma?
flashcards.back: Because a Beta blocker alone blocks Beta-2 (vasodilatory) and leaves Alpha-1 unblocked, causing vasoconstriction and a hypertensive crisis. Alpha blocker first prevents this danger

flashcards.front: What is the use of Dobutamine and why?
flashcards.back: Beta-1 agonist — increases contractility and cardiac output in acute congestive heart failure (CHF) — given intravenously

flashcards.front: What is the selectivity of Metoprolol/Atenolol and what is its condition?
flashcards.back: Beta-1 (cardiac) selectivity — but only at low doses. At high doses selectivity is lost and Beta-2 is also blocked

flashcards.front: What is Esmolol and what is its advantage?
flashcards.back: Ultra-short-acting Beta-1 blocker (half-life ~9 minutes) — IV only — for acute atrial fibrillation and cardiac rate control during surgery

flashcards.front: What are the uses of Propranolol?
flashcards.back: Hypertension, angina, arrhythmias, migraine prophylaxis, glaucoma, tremor, hyperthyroidism (reduces T4 to T3 conversion)

flashcards.front: Why are Beta blockers contraindicated in asthma?
flashcards.back: They block Beta-2, which keeps the airways open — blockade causes dangerous bronchospasm

flashcards.front: How do Beta blockers affect symptoms of hypoglycemia?
flashcards.back: They mask the adrenergic symptoms of hypoglycemia (tachycardia, tremor, palpitations). However, sweating (cholinergic, non-adrenergic) is not masked — sweating may be the only warning sign

flashcards.front: What is Mirabegron and its use?
flashcards.back: Beta-3 agonist — relaxes the detrusor muscle of the bladder — for overactive bladder (OAB) — fewer side effects than antimuscarinics

flashcards.front: What are the dose-dependent effects of Dopamine?
flashcards.back: Low: D1 (renal vasodilation) — intermediate: Beta-1 (cardiac support) — high: Alpha-1 (vasoconstriction like Norepinephrine) — the renal 'low-dose' effect is not reliably proven clinically

flashcards.front: What is the difference between Epinephrine and Norepinephrine?
flashcards.back: Epinephrine: Beta-2 effect (vasodilation) at low doses + Alpha-1 + Beta-1 at high doses — for anaphylactic shock. Norepinephrine: Alpha-1 dominates at all doses — for septic shock to raise BP

flashcards.front: What are the types of adrenergic receptors and their second messengers?
flashcards.back: α1 (Gq, ↑IP3/Ca), α2 (Gi, ↓cAMP), β1/β2/β3 (Gs, ↑cAMP).

flashcards.front: What is the effect of α1 stimulation?
flashcards.back: Vasoconstriction, mydriasis, bladder sphincter contraction.

flashcards.front: What is the effect of α2 stimulation?
flashcards.back: Presynaptic inhibition of Norepinephrine release (negative feedback) — central blood pressure reduction.

flashcards.front: What is the effect of β1 stimulation?
flashcards.back: Increased heart rate, increased cardiac contractility, and increased renin secretion.

flashcards.front: What is the effect of β2 stimulation?
flashcards.back: Bronchodilation and skeletal muscle vasodilation, uterine relaxation, increased glycogenolysis.

flashcards.front: What is the effect of β3 stimulation?
flashcards.back: Lipolysis in adipose tissue.

flashcards.front: What is the mechanism of Mirtazapine?
flashcards.back: Alpha-2 antagonist → increases Norepinephrine and serotonin release (antidepressant).

flashcards.front: What is the baroreceptor reflex?
flashcards.back: Blood pressure rise from an α1 agonist → baroreceptor stimulation → vagal activation → reflex bradycardia.

mcqs.q: A patient with asthma needs treatment for hypertension. Which beta blocker is safest?
mcqs.explanation: Metoprolol is a selective Beta-1 blocker — it may be used cautiously in patients with COPD or mild asthma because at low doses it mainly blocks cardiac Beta-1. Non-selective beta blockers such as Propranolol are contraindicated in asthma.

mcqs.q: A patient is receiving Norepinephrine IV for septic shock and it extravasates. What is the local treatment?
mcqs.explanation: Phentolamine is a reversible non-selective Alpha blocker — injected locally into the extravasation site to reverse the vasoconstriction caused by Norepinephrine and prevent tissue necrosis.

mcqs.q: A patient with pheochromocytoma is being treated. What is the correct order of drug administration?
options: Beta blocker first, then Alpha blocker
Alpha blocker first, then Beta blocker
Alpha + Beta together from the start
mcqs.explanation: Alpha blocker (e.g., Phenoxybenzamine) must be given first to prevent excessive vasoconstriction. Giving Beta blocker first would block Beta-2 vasodilation and leave Alpha-1 unblocked, causing a hypertensive crisis.

mcqs.q: What is the expected effect when Phenylephrine (Alpha-1 agonist) is given intravenously?
mcqs.explanation: Phenylephrine causes vasoconstriction and raises blood pressure. The BP rise stimulates baroreceptors, which send a signal to slow the heart compensatorily (reflex bradycardia).

mcqs.q: A patient with benign prostatic hyperplasia (BPH) has difficulty urinating. What is the most appropriate drug?
mcqs.explanation: Tamsulosin is a selective Alpha-1A blocker for prostatic and bladder receptors. It relaxes the bladder neck and prostate muscle and improves urine flow with less effect on blood pressure compared to Prazosin.

mcqs.q: What is the main warning when starting Prazosin for hypertension?
mcqs.explanation: Prazosin causes sudden vasodilation at the first dose, leading to blood pressure drop and fainting. The first dose is recommended at bedtime to avoid falls.

mcqs.q: What is the mechanism of Clonidine in lowering blood pressure?
options: Activates Alpha-2 centrally, reducing Norepinephrine release
mcqs.explanation: Clonidine is a central Alpha-2 agonist — it reduces Norepinephrine release from the CNS, decreasing sympathetic tone and lowering blood pressure.

mcqs.q: A patient with acute anaphylactic shock presents. What is the optimal drug and why?
options: Norepinephrine because it raises BP only
Epinephrine because it provides bronchodilation + vasoconstriction + cardiac support
Dobutamine because it stimulates the heart
Phenylephrine because it raises BP quickly
mcqs.explanation: Epinephrine is the drug for anaphylactic shock because it provides three necessary effects: bronchodilation (Beta-2) + vasoconstriction to raise BP (Alpha-1) + increased cardiac output (Beta-1).

mcqs.q: What are the uses of Propranolol beyond hypertension?
mcqs.explanation: Propranolol is used for: migraine prophylaxis, tremor treatment (reducing blood supply to muscles), hyperthyroidism (reduces T4→T3 conversion and alleviates adrenergic symptoms), as well as performance anxiety and glaucoma (with Timolol).

mcqs.q: A diabetic patient taking Propranolol — which warning sign of hypoglycemia will remain visible?
mcqs.explanation: Beta blockers mask the adrenergic symptoms of hypoglycemia (tachycardia, tremor, palpitations). However, sweating is governed by the cholinergic non-adrenergic system and is not masked by beta blockers — it may be the only sign.
β1 increases cardiac rate and contractility.
α2 antagonist raises Norepinephrine and serotonin.
